title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a woman called Mia Grant, who makes short cooking videos.
audio_script: Interviewer: Today I'm talking to Mia Grant, who makes short videos about cooking healthy food. Mia, did you always want to work with food? || Guest: Not really. At school I enjoyed art more than cooking, and I thought I might become a designer. My parents owned a small café, so I helped there at weekends, but it felt normal, not special. [20]I only became interested when I moved away to college and had to cook cheap meals for myself[/20]. I started taking photos of them for friends, and that slowly became videos. || Interviewer: What was the first video that many people watched? || Guest: It was a simple pasta dish. I nearly deleted it because the lighting was terrible, and I had forgotten to film the first step. But [21]people liked it because I showed how to make dinner with just five things from a small shop[/21]. Lots of students wrote to say they could actually try it. || Interviewer: Some people think online work is easy. Is that true? || Guest: No, not at all. The cooking part is enjoyable, and editing can be creative. [22]The difficult thing is planning enough ideas for every week[/22], especially when I want them to be useful and not just pretty. I keep a notebook by my bed because ideas often come late at night. || Interviewer: Do you ever get negative comments? || Guest: Yes, everyone online does. At first I read every comment and felt upset if someone didn't like a recipe. Now I'm better at ignoring rude messages. [23]What still worries me is when people change a recipe and then blame me because it doesn't work[/23]. If you use a different pan or leave something out, the result will change. || Interviewer: Have your videos changed the way you eat at home? || Guest: Yes, but not in the way people expect. I don't cook exciting meals every night. After filming all day, I usually want something very simple. [24]The biggest change is that I waste less food now[/24], because I plan carefully and use leftovers in new recipes. That has saved money too. || Interviewer: What would you like to do next? || Guest: I'd love to write a book one day, but not yet. At the moment, [25]I'm preparing a set of videos for families who want quick meals after work[/25]. I want them to be realistic, with ingredients from ordinary supermarkets and steps that children can help with. || Interviewer: How do you decide whether a recipe is good enough to share? || Guest: I test it at least twice, and I ask someone else to try it too. If they can follow the steps without asking me many questions, I know the recipe is clear. I also think about washing up, because busy people do not want to use every pan in the kitchen. Sometimes a recipe tastes good but takes too long, so I save it for a weekend idea instead. || Interviewer: Do you still enjoy cooking when it has become your job? || Guest: Yes, although I take more breaks now. I used to film late into the evening, which made me tired and less creative. Now I keep normal working hours when I can, and that helps me enjoy food again. || Interviewer: What advice would you give someone who wants to start sharing food online? || Guest: Begin with food you already cook well. Do not worry about having a perfect kitchen or expensive camera. Clear instructions and honest mistakes are more useful than a perfect picture. Also, make recipes for real people, not just for likes.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Today I'm talking to Mia Grant, who makes short videos about cooking healthy food. Mia, did you always want to work with food?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Not really. At school I enjoyed art more than cooking, and I thought I might become a designer. My parents owned a small café, so I helped there at weekends, but it felt normal, not special. I only became interested when I moved away to college and had to cook cheap meals for myself. I started taking photos of them for friends, and that slowly became videos.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What was the first video that many people watched?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: It was a simple pasta dish. I nearly deleted it because the lighting was terrible, and I had forgotten to film the first step. But people liked it because I showed how to make dinner with just five things from a small shop. Lots of students wrote to say they could actually try it.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Some people think online work is easy. Is that true?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: No, not at all. The cooking part is enjoyable, and editing can be creative. The difficult thing is planning enough ideas for every week, especially when I want them to be useful and not just pretty. I keep a notebook by my bed because ideas often come late at night.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you ever get negative comments?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, everyone online does. At first I read every comment and felt upset if someone didn't like a recipe. Now I'm better at ignoring rude messages. What still worries me is when people change a recipe and then blame me because it doesn't work. If you use a different pan or leave something out, the result will change.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Have your videos changed the way you eat at home?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, but not in the way people expect. I don't cook exciting meals every night. After filming all day, I usually want something very simple. The biggest change is that I waste less food now, because I plan carefully and use leftovers in new recipes. That has saved money too.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What would you like to do next?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I'd love to write a book one day, but not yet. At the moment, I'm preparing a set of videos for families who want quick meals after work. I want them to be realistic, with ingredients from ordinary supermarkets and steps that children can help with.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: How do you decide whether a recipe is good enough to share?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I test it at least twice, and I ask someone else to try it too. If they can follow the steps without asking me many questions, I know the recipe is clear. I also think about washing up, because busy people do not want to use every pan in the kitchen. Sometimes a recipe tastes good but takes too long, so I save it for a weekend idea instead.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you still enjoy cooking when it has become your job?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, although I take more breaks now. I used to film late into the evening, which made me tired and less creative. Now I keep normal working hours when I can, and that helps me enjoy food again.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What advice would you give someone who wants to start sharing food online?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Begin with food you already cook well. Do not worry about having a perfect kitchen or expensive camera. Clear instructions and honest mistakes are more useful than a perfect picture. Also, make recipes for real people, not just for likes.

questions.question: Why did Mia first become interested in cooking?

options.A: She wanted to become a food designer.
options.B: Her parents asked her to help in their café.
options.C: She needed to prepare meals while she was a student.

questions.explanation: Mia says she became interested when she moved away to college and had to cook cheap meals for herself.

questions.question: Why was Mia's first popular video successful?

options.A: The lighting made the food look attractive.
options.B: It was useful for people with limited ingredients.
options.C: It explained every step in great detail.

questions.explanation: Mia says people liked the video because it showed how to make dinner with just five things from a small shop.

questions.question: What does Mia find hardest about her work?

options.A: Cooking the food for the videos.
options.B: Editing the videos in an interesting way.

questions.explanation: Mia says the difficult thing is planning enough ideas for every week.

questions.question: What worries Mia about online comments?

options.A: People ask too many questions about equipment.
options.B: People are rude about the way she looks.
options.C: People blame her after changing the recipe.

questions.explanation: Mia says it still worries her when people change a recipe and then blame her because it does not work.

questions.question: How has making videos changed Mia's life at home?

options.C: She cooks special meals every evening.

questions.explanation: Mia says the biggest change is that she wastes less food.

questions.question: What is Mia working on now?
questions.explanation: Mia says she is preparing videos for families who want quick meals after work.
